coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and functionalization of red-emissive carbon dots towards sensing of copper(II) and ATP in aqueous media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bisauriya, Ramanand
Cabibbo, Marcello
Varone, Alessandra
Moro, Artur J.
Pizzoferrato, Roberto
coredata.dc:description: Red-emissive carbon dots, RCDs, were prepared in a bottom-up approach, from citric acid and urea, through a single solvothermal step, which were posteriorly surface-functionalized with di-(2-picolyl)amine (DPA) groups. The functionalized RCDs, henceforth named CD1, were analyzed through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), dynamic light-scattering (DLS) and infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and were found to be spherical, with diameter of circa 4 nm. UV-Vis and luminescence spectroscopic studies showed that CD1 display an absorption maximum at 550 nm, with emission maxima at around 640 nm. Fluorescence measurements indicated that Cu(II) induces the strongest changes in the emission of CD1 among eleven investigated metal ions, with a full quenching effect being observed. Further addition of an anionic species, adenosine 5-triphosphate (ATP), resulted in a recovery of the luminescence, which suggests that ATP is capable of sequestering metal ions from the surface of CD1. These results highlight the potential of simple and easy-to-make carbon nanomaterials as sensors for ions in aqueous media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Red emission
Copper ion
ATP

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface functionalization of red carbon dots (CD1)
Synthesis and morphological characterization of CD1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs [(a) and (b)], and statistical size distribution (c) of CD1.
ATR spectra of CD1 (black) versus RCDs prior to functionalization (red).
3D emission spectra of CD1 in miliQ water.
Emission spectra of CD1 in the presence of 20 µM of different metal ions, in 10 mM MOPS buffer at pH7 (λexc = 550 nm).
(A) Emission spectra of CD1 with increasing concentration of Cu(II), in 10 mM MOPS buffer at pH7 (λexc = 550 nm); (B) total intensity changes up to 100 µM of Cu(II).

(A) Changes in emission of CD1 in the presence of [Cd2+] = 20 µM, with increasing amounts of ATP; (B) intensity at 636 nm plotted against ATP concentration, using fixed concentration of Cd2+ at 20 µM (black dots) and 100 µM (red dots). Conditions: 10 mM MOPS buffer at pH7, λexc = 550 nm.
Synthesis and functionalization of red carbon dots, CD1.
Proposed mechanism for binding interactions of CD1 with Cd2+ and ATP.
Literature data for different CD-based sensor systems for Cu2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and functionalization of red-emissive carbon dots towards sensing of copper(II) and ATP in aqueous media

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: LAQV-REQUIMTE, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Ciências e Tecnologia, Universidade Nova de Lisboa

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: LAQV-REQUIMTE, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Ciências e Tecnologia, Universidade Nova de Lisboa, 2829-516 Caparica, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emissive carbon dots, RCDs, were prepared in a bottom-up approach, from citric acid and urea, through a single solvothermal step, which were posteriorly surface-functionalized with di-(2-picolyl)amine (DPA) groups. The functionalized RCDs, henceforth named CD1, were analyzed through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), dynamic light-scattering (DLS) and infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and were found to be spherical, with diameter of circa 4 nm. UV-Vis and luminescence spectroscopic studies showed that CD1 display an absorption maximum at 550 nm, with emission maxima at around 640 nm. Fluorescence measurements indicated that Cu(II) induces the strongest changes in the emission of CD1 among eleven investigated metal ions, with a full quenching effect being observed. Further addition of an anionic species, adenosine 5-triphosphate (ATP), resulted in a recovery of the luminescence, which suggests that ATP is capable of sequestering metal ions from the surface of CD1. These results highlight the potential of simple and easy-to-make carbon nanomaterials as sensors for ions in aqueous media.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials have been recently emerging as one of the most promising materials, given their outstanding potential in a plethora of scientific fields, allied with the availability of carbon sources that can be used in their production, including virtually all biomass and most of non-biomass waste [1]. More specifically, carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles with dimensions typically ranging from a few nanometers to tens of nanometers and with the carbon atoms arranged in a crystalline or amorphous structure. Potential applications of CDs have already been reported in electronics, medicine, catalysis, photovoltaics, energy storage, and optoelectronics [2,3]. Furthermore, their chemical flexibility makes them an attractive target for tailor-made functions, concretely at the molecular level.

In recent years, there has been a strong investment in the photophysical properties of these materials, namely due to their intrinsic fluorescent behavior, which further extends their ability as a promising alternative to silica nanoparticles bearing fluorescent dyes, or semiconductor quantum dots [4,5]. However, the fluorescence mechanism of CDs is not fully understood since these nanoparticles, differently from semiconductor quantum dots, do not necessarily exhibit quantum confinement and simple size-dependent behavior. Instead, their optical properties are mostly influenced by the composition of the surface and the defects in the π-conjugated core structure [6,7]. In fact, multiple fluorescence emission centers, including defect-related core states and quasi-molecular surface states, seem to contribute to the energy transfer and fluorescence emission processes [8,9]. While this situation calls for more investigation, it allows researchers to use a semi-empirical approach to achieve a great tunability of fluorescence spectra, which can be tailored from the UV to the near-infrared (NIR), and improve quantum efficiency [10,11] For instance, much attention has been paid to emission in the visible range for Light-Emitting Diode (LED) applications [12].

In addition, the fluorescence emission of carbon dots generally depends on the presence and interaction with other chemical or biological species, and this has strongly boosted investigations on the sensing applications of these carbon nanoparticles [13,14]. Sensitivity to heavy metals (HMs) is easily achieved in CDs due the intrinsic presence of various oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface and their reactivity towards metal cations [15]. Response to a huge number of other analytes, however, has been obtained through chemical modifications of the carbon structure and/or doping with heteroatoms [16]. In fact, to a certain extent, sensitivity towards a specific class of analytes can be produced and tuned by using appropriate methods of synthesis and functionalization.

Indeed, the preparation of carbon dots using either top-down or bottom-up approaches has already been strongly demonstrated [17,18]. Most common top-down approaches include chemical exfoliation (strong acids and high temperature) [19], laser ablation [20], and ultrasonication [21] from various carbon sources, such as coal and organic biomass from vegetables or fruits, etc. Bottom-up preparation of carbon dots can be performed via microwave radiation, or by a hydrothermal/solvothermal method, depending on whether the solvent is aqueous-based or not [22].

Based on these previous works, we envisaged to produce red-emissive sensor carbon dots (RCDs) with increased selectivity by post-functionalization with di-(2-picolyl)-amine (DPA), a widely used chelating moiety towards metals, particularly Zn(II) [23,24] and Cu(II) [25]. Producing carbon dots with optical properties shifted towards the red region of the electromagnetic spectrum (as opposed to the majority of these systems which report emission in the blue or green) is key to broaden the applications of these materials to biological Sciences, as this allows deeper tissue penetration/monitoring and prevents/minimizes auto-fluorescence of biosamples [26]. Furthermore, metal coordinated-DPA has also been widely reported for the detection of anionic species, with emphasis on phosphate derivatives [27]. In this way, we intended to combine the remarkable, stable fluorescence properties of RCDs with the specific affinity of DPA to metal ions, with the possibility of extending their use to detect anionic species. The newly designed materials were fully characterized with respect to their sensing properties.

All used chemicals were of analytical grade and used as purchased, and were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), Carlo Erba (Barcelona, Spain), Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA) or TCI Europe (Zwijndrecht, Belgium). In detail: N,N-dimethylformamide - DMF (Carlo Erba, ACS grade, 99.9 %), ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich, analytical grade, ≥99.8 %), citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, >99.5 %), urea (Alfa Aesar, 99–100.5 %), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride - EDC.HCl (Sigma-Aldrich), 4-(dimethylamino)pyridine (Sigma-Aldrich, Reagent Plus© grade, 99 %), 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole hydrate – HOBt (Sigma-Aldrich, 97 %). For titrations, Sodium 3-morpholinopropane-1-sulfonate (MOPS, Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99 %) was used as buffer, adenosine 5’triphosphate disodium salt hydrate (ATP) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (99 %), and metal ions were all used in the form of their corresponding perchlorate salts: sodium perchlorate (Sigma-Aldrich, ACS reagent grade, ≥98 %), magnesium perchlorate (Sigma-Aldrich, ACS reagent grade), lithium perchlorate (Sigma-Aldrich, ACS reagent grade, ≥95 %), cadmium perchlorate hydrate (Sigma-Aldrich), potassium perchlorate (Sigma-Aldrich, ACS reagent grade, +99 %), iron perchlorate (Sigma-Aldrich, 98 %), calcium perchlorate tetrahydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %), copper perchlorate hexahydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, 98 %), zinc perchlorate hexahydrate (Sigma-Aldrich), nickel perchlorate hexahydrate (Alfa Aesar, reagent grade), lead perchlorate trihydrate (Alfa Aesar, 97–102 %) and cobalt perchlorate hexahydrate (Alfa Aesar, reagent grade). The synthesis of N,N-bis(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine (DPA-ligand 2) was performed in two steps, following previously reported procedures [28]. Further details can be found in SI.

RCDs were synthesized by following the reported procedure based on typical hydrothermal method [29]. Briefly, a large amount production was carried out by making a solution of the precursors, 4 g citric acid and 8 g urea in 40 mL N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), sonicating them to complete dissolution and transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave which was heated at 180 ºC for 4 h, followed by the natural cooling down the reactor. The resulting product was then mixed with 80 mL ethanol and centrifuged at 2400 g for 20 minutes. The above process of centrifugation was repeated twice to wash away residual solvents and small organic molecules.

This product has the additional fluorophore, in the green region in addition to the red ones, that were eliminated by dissolving the precipitate first in sodium hydroxide solution (50 mg/mL), followed with sonication for 15 min and centrifuged at 2400 g for 30 min, and then secondly the collected precipitate was further dissolved in hydrochloric solution (5 wt%) with sonication for 15 min and centrifugation at 2400 g for 30 min. The obtained precipitation was re-dissolved in miliQ water and centrifuged at 2400 g for 30 min twice to remove remaining hydrochloric acid. The precipitate was dried at room temperature in desiccator and stored at 4 ºC for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functionalization of red carbon dots (CD1)
ce:section.ce:para: As part of our interest in sensing field, red fluorescent and rich in carboxylic acid RCDs discussed above were further functionalized through NHS-ester activation. In this sense, we activated carboxylic groups from RCDs with EDC.HCl and DMAP, in the presence of a catalytic amount of HOBt, followed by the introduction of DPA-ligand 2, in good yields [30].

In brief, the procedure for amide coupling started with a stirred solution of DPA-ligand 2 (20.1 mg, 0.82 µmol, 1 equivalent) in a solution of RCDs (0.7 mg/mL, total of 10 mL), EDC.HCl (15.9 mg, 0.82 µmol, 1 equivalent), HOBt (5 mg, 0.37 µmol, 0.45 equivalent) and DMAP (10.1 mg, 0.82 µmol, 1 equivalent) were added at 0 °C. The resulting mixture was stirred at 23 °C for 48 h followed by a dialysis (benzoylated dialysis tubing, from Sigma-Aldrich) against milli Q water overnight to obtain what will hereafter be called stock solution of CD1.

Dynamic light-scattering (DLS) measurements were performed on a Nanoparticle Analyzer SZ-100 (Horiba Scientific, Kyoto, Japan), in 3 mL (10 mm × 10 mm, width × depth) fully transparent PMMA cells. Prior to each measurement, the samples were filtered using a 0.46 µm polystyrene membrane disc filter. Zeta-potential (ζ-potential) experiments were performed on the same instrument but using specific microelectronic capillary cuvettes (100 µL, Horiba Scientific, Kyoto, Japan). Calculation of ζ-potential for all samples was made using the Smoluchowski model. All measurements from both DLS and ζ-potential were performed in triplicate.

Electron-transparent and thermally stable samples for TEM analysis were obtained from droplets of CD1 solution dropped on a carbon film and thermally stabilized by IR exposition to a duration ranging 30–100 s. A double-tilt nitrogen cooled TEM specimen holder was used. TEM inspections were performed by using a Philips™ CM-20® (Philips, The Netherlands; Amsterdam) working at 0.2 MV. Statistical evaluation of the carbon dot mean size and spatial distribution was carried out on more than 350 individual CD1 measurements, using Leica™ (Leica Microsystems GmbH, Germany; Wetzlar) Image pro-plus® software.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected in the 2Θ angular range 5–30° by using the Mo-Kα radiation (λ = 0.70930 Å) with 2Θ angular steps of 0.05° and counting time of 5 s per step. The crystalline phase identification was carried out through the JCPDS-ICDD database (41–1487).

Solutions for UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence measurements were prepared by adding an aliquot of 40 µL of the stock solution of CD1 (0.7 mg/mL) to 3 mL of miliQ water or aqueous buffer solution (stock of CD1 was always ultrasonicated for 10 minutes at room temperature, prior to addition). For all metal titrations, 10 mM MOPS [Sodium 3-morpholinopropane-1-sulfonate] buffer at pH 7.1 ± 0.2 was used. Metal ion titrations were performed by adding a stock solution of metal to a cuvette containing CD1, ensuring that the added volume did not exceed 10 % of the total volume. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined according to IUPAC guidelines [31,32], by measuring five independently prepared samples of CD1 with no metal (blank) and applying the formula: LOD = 3σ/b, where σ represents the standard deviation of these measurements, and b represents the slope over a fixed linear range (0 – 5 µM was selected). Absorption spectra were acquired in a 1 cm quartz cuvette on a Varian Cary 100 Bio UV- spectrophotometer. Emission spectra were obtained in a 1 cm fluorescence quartz cuvette, using a Horiba-Jobin-Yvon SPEX FL3–2iHR spectrofluorometer. Fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CD1 was measured using Nile Red in ethanol (QY = 52 %)[33], as reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphological characterization of CD1
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, carbon dots were prepared via a bottom-up approach, using citric acid and urea as the starting materials. Hydrothermal synthesis methodology, followed by several washings with water, acid and base treatment, yielded a dark powder consisting of carbon nanodots. Taking advantage of the fact that the surface is covered with carboxylic acid groups, further functionalization was performed by covalently linking previously synthesized N,N-bis(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)ethane-1,2-diamine (DPA ligand 2) in water at room temperature (see experimental section for details) through NHS-ester activation with EDC, to produce the final carbon dots, CD1 ( Scheme 1).

TEM was used to determine the typical size and morphology of the red carbon dots. In this regard, Fig. 1 shows representative TEM micrographs of CD1 where typical sizes can be observed (Fig. 1a). Selected-Area Electron Diffraction Pattern (SAEDP) of nanosized carbon dots, and the carbon dots to which it refers, is reported in figure S3. Lattice spacing was measured as 0.26 nm, as reported in the micrograph of Fig. 1b. Fig. 1c reports the statistical evaluation of the CD1 sizes. It appeared that the mean size of the carbon dots was 4.0 ± 1.2 nm. The size distribution was not too large, being in the range 1.1 – to – 8.2 nm. As reported in the TEM micrographs of Fig. 1b, CD1 appeared to be as uniform black dots, with no discontinuity from the core to the outer zone and characterized by a typical spherical morphology. The lattice distance of 0.26 nm is quite close to the distance between the carbon lattice planes (100).

DLS measurements are reasonably consistent with HR-TEM images, revealing an average size diameter around 2.62 ± 0.07 nm (fig. S4), with a relatively good polydispersity index (PDI = 0.227 ± 0.139). In order to get information exclusively on the core of CD1, XRD was performed upon solvent evaporation and deposition on a stainless-steel sample-holder. The XRD pattern is reported in Figure S5 of the Supplementary Materials and is characterized by the typical broad peak centered approximately at 2θ = 10° that is a key feature of most carbon dots obtained by various synthesis methods [34,35]. This broad peak is generally ascribed to carbonaceous materials with a significant broadening attributed to the small size of the domains and the arrangement of disordered carbon atoms within the hexagonal graphite structure, which produces the formation of turbostratic structures through interlayer packing [35]. An additional crystalline feature is observed at 2θ = 13°, likely due to a graphitization process occurred during the solvent evaporation and deposition (performed on hot plate) for measurement purposes.
Carbon dots were further analyzed with Attenuated Transmission Reflectance (ATR), and the results are presented in Fig. 2.

As one can observe, the functionalized CD1 present more absorption peaks in the infrared region of the electromagnetic spectrum than the non-functionalized ones. There are two bands which are shared between the two samples: (a) a broad signal between 3100 and 3400 cm−1, which is likely to correspond to the OH groups from carboxylic acids that remain in the carbon dot matrix; and (b) a peak at around 1650 cm−1, which can be attributed to the carbonyl stretch from the amide groups of the urea matrix. Carbon dots that were further functionalized with chelating group 2 present several other signals, namely: (a) a series of peaks at 3000–2950 cm−1 region, corresponding to aliphatic C-H stretch from the several CH2 groups; (b) a series of peaks in the region of 1480–1270 cm−1, which may be attributed to asymmetric bending and scissoring from C-H bonds; (c) two sharp peaks that arise from the C-N stretching of the amine in 2; and (d) a peak at circa 880 cm−1, characteristic of C-H bending from aromatic groups. This distinction between the two samples is an indication that 2 is indeed present within the matrix of CD1.

The optical properties of CD1 were further studied, and the UV-Vis absorption spectra were acquired in aqueous media. The absorption profile revealed a single band in the visible region centered at around 550 nm. Excitation at this wavelength yielded an emission band with maximum at circa 640 nm (fig. S6). In order to fully ascertain the emissive properties of CD1, a 3D emission spectrum was acquired, ranging from the UV to the far red ( Fig. 3)

The exhibited profile reveals that there are essentially two sets of fluorophores/emissive species in the carbon dots matrix, with maxima centered at 460 and 640 nm, being this latter responsible for the strongest emission. For this reason (and to have a better sensitivity), we pursued the remainder of the studies by monitoring solely the red emission band (∼640 nm), which exhibited a quantum yield of QY = 3 % for excitation at 550 nm.

As mentioned above, the carbon dots were functionalized with a di-(2-picolyl)amine (DPA) moiety (see structure 2, Scheme 1). Indeed, DPA is a well-known chelating group, capable of binding several metal ions, including Zn(II) [24,36], Cd(II) [37] and Cu(II) [38]. As such, the sensitivity of CD1 was tested against several metal ions, by adding a concentrated aliquot of a metal stock solution to dilute solution of CD1. The results are summarized in Fig. 4.

In detail, we can distinguish three different behaviours: (1) metal ions that lead to a strong luminescent quenching (≥75 %) and a ∼20 nm hypsochromic shift, which include Cu(II), Ni(II) and Co(II); (2) metal ions that induce quenching up to 60 %, namely Pb(II), Cd(II) and Zn(II), although these two latter with a stronger hypsochromic shift in the emission maximum (∼23 nm); (3) metals that do not affect the luminescent of CD1, namely Fe(II), Cr(VI, Ca(II), Na(I) and Mg(II). These findings show that the selectivity of CD1 is indeed quite similar to several known systems that possess the same DPA chelating moiety [24,39].

In order to gain further insight on the sensitivity of CD1, we performed titrations for a selection of the metal ions that induced some luminescent response. The results for Cu(II) are shown in Fig. 5, while those for Cd(II) and Pb(II) are presented in SI (Figs. S7 and S8).

Copper(II) is by far the strongest quencher in the emission properties of carbon dots CD1, exhibiting almost complete quenching when reaching 100 µM. In addition, the hypsochromic shift induced by Cu(II) in the emission is the largest one and can easily be observed by naked eye at concentrations as low as 2 µM, as displayed in Figure S9d-f. This behavior is consistent with many DPA-based systems where Cu(II) induces fluorescence quenching, which is essentially related to: (1) the high binding constants towards Cu(II) typically exhibited by DPA; and (2) the fact that Cu(II) is a d9 transition metal with a tendency to open excited state de-excitation pathways via electronic energy transfer and/or photoinduced electron transfer [40]. The limits of detection (LODs) for the different metals that induce response from CD1 were determined according to IUPAC guidelines [31,32], and are summarized in table S1 (see also figure S10). The values are in line with comparable sensing systems already in the literature, particularly towards Cu2+, with the advantage of possessing optical properties shifted to the red region of the electromagnetic spectrum, as mentioned above ( Table 1).

The practical applicability of this material was tested by carrying out the standard sensing experiment on spiked samples of real tap water and lake water. Specifically, tap water was obtained from the city water mains of Rome (in the Department of Industrial Engineering of University of Rome Tor Vergata) while lake water was collected from the volcanic lake of Castel Gandolfo, near Rome. The results are reported in Table S2 and show that the percentage recovery in the lake water was in the range from 95 % to 97 %. This is reasonably satisfactory, especially when considering that the water samples did not undergo any treatment after collection, but simple mechanical filtration through 0.22 µm filter to remove larger organic particles. Significantly worse results were obtained in the tap water samples, with the recovery varying from 90 % to 110 % and this might be due to the fact that drinking water of Rome exhibits high permanent hardness (33 fH), with a calcium content as high as 101 mg/L (2.5 mM).

Zeta-potential experiments were also performed in CD1 samples, to assess the surface-charge of the carbon dots (fig. S11). Without any metal ions, CD1 carbon dots exhibit a negative surface charge of −30.8 (±2.1) mV, most likely due to remaining unfunctionalized carboxylic acid groups, which are deprotonated at pH7. Upon addition of 100 µM of Cu(II) ions, a marked shift in the surface charge takes place, with a registered positive value of 8.9 (±0.6) mV, which is a clear indication of positively charged metal ions onto the surface of CD1.

Coordinated DPA-M2+ compounds, in particular when the metal ion is Zn2+ or Cd2+, have been widely reported as structures capable of sensing anionic species, namely phosphate derivatives [27]. With this in mind, further experiments were conducted on CD1 to assess their potential for this purpose, by adding adenosine 5’-triphosphate (ATP) after metal binding. The resulting spectra are shown in Fig. 6, for the case of Cd2+-bound to CD1. Equivalent experiments were also performed for CD1-Cu2+ and CD1-Pb2+ (figs. S12 and S13).

As we can see from the corresponding emission spectra, increasing ATP concentrations lead to an increase in the fluorescent signal from CD1 (Fig. 6B). The same experiment was performed with a higher metal ion content (Fig. 6B, red dots) and, in this case, we observe no changes in the maximum fluorescence intensity up to a concentration of Cd(II) of ∼100 µM, at which point an increase in the emission takes place. This indicates that, in fact, ATP molecules do not bind to the DPA-metal coordination sphere. Instead, ATP anions are actually binding to free Cd2+ ions in solution preferentially and, when this excess metal is no longer free in solution, then ATP sequesters the Cd2+ from the surface of CD1 nanodots, with a concomitant recovery of the initial emission from CD1 ( Scheme 2).

This effect has already been previously reported for similar DPA chemosensor systems [38], and is consistent with the relatively high stability constants of ATP with metal ions [50]. Analogous experiments made with 20 µM of other metal ions and excess ATP indicate a similar effect for the remaining studied metal ions (fig. S14).

In order to test the stability of CD1 against ageing, fluorescence and sensing tests were repeated after 9 months, with the stock solution stored at 4 °C in refrigerator, and again after further 1 week of storing at RT. As reported in Figures S15 and S16, and in Table S3, the material still retained approximately the same quantum efficiency (within 95 %) and performance towards Cu2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and ATP (within 90 %) which accounts for its stability.

Red-emissive carbon dots were successfully synthesized through hydrothermal method, followed by surface functionalization with DPA metal chelating units. The average size of the particles was determined to be around 4 nm, and their optical properties revealed an absorption maximum at 550 nm, with emission peaking at circa 640 nm. Fluorescence screening studies against eleven different metal ions indicated a higher sensitivity towards Cu(II), with a LOD of 160 nM, comparable to other known sensor systems. Further interactions with ATP show that these carbon dots may also be used for detection of anions via displacement of the metal ion. This study confirmed that functionalization of CDs with metal-chelating moieties is a promising and successfully strategy to enhance the great versatility of this class of carbon nanomaterials, especially in the field of sensing applications.

Ramanand Bisauriya: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Marcello Cabibbo: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Artur J. Moro: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Roberto Pizzoferrato: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Alessandra Varone: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work received support and help from FCT/MCTES (LA/P/0008/2020 DOI 10.54499/LA/P/0008/2020, UIDP/50006/2020 DOI 10.54499/UIDP/50006/2020 and UIDB/50006/2020 DOI 10.54499/UIDB/50006/2020), through Portuguese national funds. This work was also supported by Regione Lazio through Progetto di Ricerca POR FESR LAZIO 2014–2020 Id: A0375–2020–36403 according to G09493 del 14 July 2021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Seafood waste derived carbon nanomaterials for removal and detection of food safety hazards
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: He Z., Lin H., Sui J., Wang K., Wang H., Cao L. Seafood waste derived carbon nanomaterials for removal and detection of food safety hazards. Science of The Total Environment 2024:172332. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.172332.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in red-emissive carbon dots and their biomedical applications
Mater. Chem. Front.
